lg.l: Apart from the girl of the people of the middle [region], with the great joy
Of having obtained [the privilege of giving] refuge to the doll-girl difficult to obtain,
[And] the seat of the cowherds surrounded by thicket/an enclosure
Along with cowherdresses of food/eating [from] selling buttermilk,
[Under] an eminent, crafted roof that was fed with red earth,
Having housed [Kaṇṇaki] in a protected house [which was] a protected, small house,
Together with a few cowherdresses with close-set bangles,
[Mātari] bowing, saying, ‘For You who has come with an un-made [= natural] beauty
[As if] in order to destroy the rare costliness of fresh ornnaments of gold
By which the women of Kūṭal obtain [their] beauty, look at my daughter Aiyai.
[She is you] subordinate/servant-woman. I hid [you like] a beautiful flower-garland
As though [you were] gold. Stay here with me, oh lady.’ [Further] saying,
The lady of great penance, having removed [your] suffering [from] the journey,
Has placed [you] [in] a place of no [further] distress.’ Saying,
‘Is there worry for your man? Now, since [he] is a venerable one
Of the Jain ascetics [= he follows their spiritual path], [ladies!]
Grant without delay the good vessels that are appropriate for
making [= cooking] rice for worshipping [for] the day,
To the lady [who is your] brother’s wife.’ Saying, ‘Oh ladies,
Obtain, along with the cooking vessels that are undiminished [in their] nature
[Like in the custom of] the clans-women of the middle [region], the round unripe fruit [from]
The rich fruit of the Kōḷippākal jackfruit with an eminent usage;
The curved, unripe fruit of pomegranate [with] light lines along with
The fruit of of mango [with] green unripe fruit, [and] paddy grains
[With] sweet fruit, along with the ‘fruit’ [= produce] of your milk!’
Giving [these things to Kaṇṇaki], as [Kaṇṇaki] cut up with a bent tipped knife/sickle
The green, unripe fruits of many types, so that [her] tender fingers reddened,
[Her] brilliant face sweat. [Her already] red eyes [further] reddened.
As she who watched the kitchen [stove with] a black outer rim moved [away],
Along with Aiyai who kindled the keen flame, [Kaṇṇaki] having made
For [her] loving man [a meal] with cooking of a well-trained hand,
After the man [= Kōvalaṉ] sat on a mat of craftwork that was woven beautifully
[By] the women [with] skilled hands with the pure, white fronds of palmyra grass,
[Kaṇṇaki] [being] she who worshipped [the feet by] having cleansed the feet
Of [her] lover [with] water from a terracotta bowl, with pretty hands [like] fragrant blossoms,
Like she who dispelled the fatigue of the Goddess of the earth,
Splashing cool water, rubbing [his feet] with her hands, spreading the young leaf
Of a young banana plant, [in] this way, saying, ‘Venerable one,
Please eat [this] ambrosia [in] this way.’ Completing in one [= the right] manner
All the befitting processes with respect to the rare Vedas for the [caste of]
Those who are after the king[ly caste], [the cowherdresses] saying,
Isn’t this the man of the colour of a new, bilberry blossom that was raised
Having been birthed, by Acōtai in the cowherd village (Āyarpāṭi),
[This] Lord over here who eats the good ambrosia?
And this lady [with] many bangles, isn’t she the lamp that ended
The distresses of Him [with] a colour of pure sapphires
From the ancient, good lineage, in the Toḻuṉai (Yamunā) river?’
Aiyai and [her] mother [Mātari] reaching [a state of] admiration,
Saying, ‘The sight of him [in] this way is unfathomable [to] the eyes!’
[Kōvalaṉ] embracing [the lady with] dark, wet [= oiled] tresses saying ‘come!’
Who offered the unripe fruit of betel along with a roll of pretty, tender betel leaves
To the superior, great man who sat pleasantly, having eaten,
Saying, ‘The tender feet of the girl - would they be able, even a little bit,
To overcome the roads [with] stones and sand?’ Saying, ‘Being aggrieved
What did my old parents experience for [our] having gone into
The difficult desert [like] a hot battlefield? Is it illusion? Is it strong Karma?
I know nothing, [my] heart heart having been stirred up. Entering into
Long jokes [with] groups of people [with] trivial words, with
People of speech [and] with new rabble-rousers, consuming aimless talk,
Is there a good path for I who killed troublesome [behaviour among]
Those who speak of [only] wealth? I failed the command of both [our] old parents
And I created distress to the little one less in age [but] ancient [in knowledge].
I did not see [it] as a deviation [from the righteous path]. You [= Kaṇṇaki]
Got up when [I] said ‘Get up [from] here, to the great city [of Maturai].
As she [Kaṇṇaki] said, ‘To me who lost [the roles of]
Taking care of people of virtue, protecting Brahmins, receiving ascetics
And receiving [and] providing for guests, the man of great justice [= Kōvalaṉ’s father]
Of royal, great excellence [with] great efforts [that come with] a great name
Along with the great lady [= Kōvalaṉ’s mother], having mingled [with me through] graceful words,
[Their] loving hearts being superior, the hearts that did not resemble
Setting [me aside] [because] of hatred [towards me, I] not having stood before [them],
Praising my [resilience], you [Kōvalaṉ] desired to behave in an unpraisworthy way,
So that [my] inner heart that is hidden by a false smile suffers, as though [still]
Declaring the pain and distress that I had concealed in [my] heart.
Because I am one of a life [with] a heart that does not change,
I got up [and] departed [with you to Maturai].’ [Kōvalaṉ] saying,
‘[You] having left behind [your] kinfolk starting [with your] family,
The little, young ones, [your] servants, confidantes [and your] companions,
Bashfulness, naivité and chastity that nurtures, which good people praise
Being [your only] great companions, having come [here] with me,
Oh gold[en one] who removed my misery! Oh creeper!
Oh beautiful flower-garland! Oh doll[-like girl] of humility!
Oh lamp [to] the vast earth! Oh tender sprout of marital duties!
Oh prosperous one of elegance! I, taking one of the anklets [on your] little feet,
Having exchanged [it for money], will come. Stay behind [= here] without worrying.’
Embracing altogether [= with his whole body] his lady-love [with] long eyes [like]
Black carps, seeing [her state of being] all alone/her solutitude, without anyone close [to her],
His inner heart being perturbed, becoming one with eyes that hid incoming tears,
Leaving the house of the cowherd [with] many cows, he who would go to the streets
With a walk that was not strong, he was one who did not know that
A humped bull facing [him] was a fault/evil [= bad omen] because it was not
A befitting thing [i.e., custom] known to his clan. [Kōvalaṉ] having crossed over
The village common [with] fine dung [on the floor] together [along with that bull],
Walking to the middle of the street [with] beautiful [ladies], he would go
To the street [with] shop counters. Over there, as a hundred smiths of fine work
That was completed by hand, came behind [a group of] craftsmen,
[Kōvalaṉ] being one who saw [among them] a smith [with] a stick [in his] hand
Going [with] a transverse [= wide] gait, wearing a shirt, putting together that
This man is the smith who works with gold, who obtained superiority
With the name of the southern one [= the Pāṇṭiya king], [Kōvalaṉ] asking,
‘Would you be the primary [one] for the setting of the price of one foot ornament
For the eminent lady of the guardian [= Pāṇṭiya king]?’ As the messenger of Yama
‘Alas! Even though I do not know [the answer], I [do] make the jewels
[For] the king, starting [with his] crown.’ [Kōvalaṉ then] opened
The mouth [= clasp] of the covering of the anklet that is eludes praise.
The gold smith of dishonest work having looked desirously (?) at
All the workmanship of the picturesque anklet [of Kōvalaṉ] — an anklet
Of the finest gold, with rows of gem-sockets to which diamonds were attached
Along with sapphires [befitting of being] crown-jewels, [the smith]
Going forward, thinking/saying [to Kōvalaṉ], ‘There is no befittingness of this anklet
Apart from to the royal, great lady [= Pāṇṭiya queen]’, saying, ‘I will come,
Having explained [the existence of this anklet] to the king of much victory.’
Saying, ‘[Kōvalaṉ!] Stay over there [in] the small hut.’ After [Kōvalaṉ]
Entered into the wing of a god’s temple at the seat/home of the lowly man,
[The smith], saying, ‘I will make go [the false story of] the foot-ornament that
I have secretly taken [= stolen] along with the new one who has moved [From a different] land
To the king, before [the information of my deceit] is revealed, having spread’
He who concealed a heart that is unmoveable [= merciless] would [thus] go [to the king].
[The Pāṇṭiya queen], having concealed within [herself her] sulking heart,
Thinking, ‘the conviction of the dance [by] the ladies of Kūṭal, the sections of song
And the fruit of the melody have captured the heart of the guardian [= king]’,
As the eminent lady [= queen, who was] the base of the [royal] family moved away
Having put upon herself [= feigned] suffering [from] a headache, the king,
Having left his circle of ministers/advisors, looking at the palace of the royal, great lady
With servant girls [with] long eyes with overflowing [red] streaks,
[The goldsmith] being one who fell [to the ground] within the limit of the eye [= at a distance]
[At] the end of the entrance with a guard, lying [there], bowing [and] praising [the king] manifold,
Saying, ‘Even without a crowbar, or a forked staff, [Kōvalaṉ] taking as a companion
A spell that he considered [in his mind], inducing the guardsmen into a drunken sleep’,
Saying, ‘The thief who took the palace’s anklet having hidden from watchmen of
[This] bustling/loud, big town, has been staying inside my little, small hut,
Because it was the time of Karma coming to fruition,
The man with the margosa [flowers] that blossom on branches [= the Pāṇṭiya king]
Was one to whom [this fact was] quite unclear.’ As the guadian [= the Pāṇṭiya king]
Ordered, having called the guards of the town, if the anklet of the foot of my
[Lady like] a hanging, flower-garland, is in the hand of the [now-]injured thief,
Bring here that anklet, having killed [the thief].’ And because the smith of evil deeds
Had accomplished the thing he had wished for, with a heart that ordered [him],
Being one who crossed over [= approached] Kōvalaṉ, who had fallen into,
Having gone into the all-surrounding net of fiery Karma,
As the [Pāṇṭiya] king ordered [his] mighty army; the goldsmith of false work
Showing desirously those who came to see the anklet [= the guardsmen of the king]
All the makings of the well-crafted anklet, saying, ‘He [Kōvalaṉ] [is the one]’,
Having spoken [words of insult], smiling in a concealed way [= a false smile]
At the people [= the guardsmen] of rare powers who said, ‘He who sits like this,
By way of [his] appearance, is no boy who kills’, the smith of evil work
Would [then] narrate, being one who showed [Kōvalaṉ to the guards]:
‘If you fell [victim] to a drug/poison, from moving [with] people
Of a lowly life of plunder as [your] company, not to mention the eight things —
Mantras, [evil] gods/spirits, drugs, omens, trickery, [strange] places,
[strange] times [and] instruments/weapons — all of you have also fallen
To the great wrath of the king with the great name! If it is the horribly enmity
Of a mantra-chanting tongue, like the sons of Indra, I couldn’t see [them], could I,
If they become transparent [like] the appearance of a God?
They would escape, even after revealing the [stolen] object in [their very] hand!
Over there, if they confuse with a drug, who is there in the place to come
[To] us who sit [transfixed]? Unless if an opportune moment arises,
They will not desire [our] rare wealth, even if it comes, entering [their] hands.
[However], if they think of an act of trickery, they will acquire
Even the garland on Intiraṉ’s chest! If this place is the place [they decided on]
For the giving away [=taking] of this wealth, who will have seen them [= the thieves]
In that place? If the wealth is in the hands of those who examined the [opportune] time,
Would obstructing [them] happen, even if it is [by] elders/superiors?
If rare wealth is in the hands of those who possess weapons,
Who would have seen them now, in [this] big world? [To such thieves],
The two that are day and night do not exist. If one asks for a place to hide [from the thieves],
There will be no place to enter [= to seek refuge in.]
Having lingered at the entrance in disguise of a messenger,
Having entered [the palace] in a beautiful/woman’s disguise,
He who does not hesitate having gone [inside] in the shadows of lamps,
Taking, like a flash [of lightning], the light-emitting diamonds
[From] the swaying, bright garland of the young prince,
[The prince, being] one who opened [his] sleeping eyes, did not see
[The garland] on [his] shoulders. Unsheathing the sword [in his] posession,
Taking the sheath [in his] hand, he did not cry out to the nature in which
[The thief] restrained [the sword] amidst blows [by the prince].
Seeing [each other] in a [swordless] wrestling match,
As the smith of evil deeds spoke, saying, ‘If there is someone
Who saw the thief that moved with expertise, show him !
In the world, is there one equal to this one [= the thief] here?’
Over there, a young man [with] broad hands [with] a perfect spear
Would speak: ‘As one guy appeared at night [when] the town was asleep,
— A guy with a chisel [that could] break the ground [who was]
An army man in blue [= dark clothes], out of desire for jewels
Being like a swift tiger in the obscurity of of the strong darkness
Of a rainy midnight, as I unsheathed the sword [in my] hand,
As he grasped the sword [in] my hand, I could not see him
In any of the entrances! His actions are rare. And, the king will be vexed
There is [but] one suitable action. Oh you armed men, tell [us what to do].’
One guy — an uneducated, intoxicated boy — hurled [his] white sword.
It ruptured [Kōvalaṉ through] the middle, horizontally. As blood
Shooting forth from the wound flowed while spreading,
As the sky-high misery of the lady of the earth increased,
Kōvalaṉ fell, so that the guardian’s straight sceptre [of justice] folded,
Ancient, [now] ripe Karma having taken [its] form.
